cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure out this problem. It's about four students who each get a different number from a set S of four consecutive two-digit integers. The teacher tells them that one number is divisible by 6 and another by 7. Initially, none of them can figure out the set S, but after hearing that, they all can. We need to find the sum of all possible values of the greatest element of S. First, let me parse the problem again to make sure I understand all the constraints. - Set S has four consecutive two-digit integers. So, for example, something like {a, a+1, a+2, a+3} where each is between 10 and 99 inclusive. - Each student gets a different number from S. So they each have one unique number from the set. - The teacher says that "some number in S was divisible by 6, and a different number in S was divisible by 7." So, in S, there is at least one multiple of 6 and at least one multiple of 7, and these are different numbers (i.e., not the same number divisible by both 6 and 7). - The teacher asks if any of the students could deduce S. All four students say "no." - Then, upon hearing that all four said "no," each student can determine S. So, we need to find all such sets S where this logic happens, and then sum their greatest elements. First step: Let's consider all possible sets S of four consecutive two-digit integers. But we also know that in S, there must be a multiple of 6 and a multiple of 7.
